text_blocks.text: According to one of her
biographers, Giles St Aubyn, Victoria wrote
an average of 2,500 words a day during
her adult life. From July 1832 until just
before her death, she kept a detailed
journal, which eventually encompassed 122
volumes. After Victoria's death, her youngest
daughter, Princess Beatrice, was appointed
her literary executor. Beatrice transcribed
and edited the diaries covering Victoria's
accession onwards, and burned the originals
in the process. Despite this destruction,
much of the diaries still exist. In addition to
Beatrice's edited copy, Lord Esher
transcribed the volumes from 1832 to 1861
before Beatrice destroyed them. Part of
Victoria's extensive correspondence has been
published in volumes edited by A. C.
Benson, Hector Bolitho, George Earle
Buckle, Lord Esher, Roger Fulford, and
Richard Hough among others.
